previewBanner: Design preview — mirrors the production homepage. Route / is the shipped landing.

hero.title: Vyntrio
hero.description: Boot install media on your own hardware into a local setup dashboard. Own the appliance path — not a rented cloud console.
hero.ctaDownloadHint: USB image, checksums, and Media Creator
hero.ctaSignInHint: For hosts that already run Vyntrio

heroStory.ariaLabel: Appliance signal path from hardware to control center

trustBand.heading: Built for people who keep data
trustBand.intro: Vyntrio is shaped for local appliances — not rented control planes. These marks describe the operating model, not partner sponsorships.

liveRelease.heading: Install media and checksums
liveRelease.intro: Live values from this host’s release staging API. Empty fields mean the artifact is not staged here — not a fake download.

appsVms.heading: Where containers and VMs sit in the product
appsVms.intro: TrueNAS/Unraid-class products eventually include apps and VMs. Vyntrio states the gap plainly: those layers are not shipping yet.
appsVms.appsBody: No app catalog, no container runtime UI, and no compose deploy path in the current appliance. Roadmap docs are not product capability.
appsVms.vmsBody: No in-product hypervisor manager. Dev-only install-media VM helpers are not a VM product.

installJourney.heading: From install media to a running appliance
installJourney.intro: Four phases from first boot to a reachable admin surface. Media and step-by-step detail ship with the public release — the flow stays the same.

steps.title: Create boot media
steps.body: Write the install image to USB or equivalent and verify against the published checksum — only once the image exists.

steps.title: Install on target hardware
steps.body: EFI-capable x86_64 host as the appliance target. Partitioning and system setup run in the installer — not a container-only deployment.

steps.title: Network and reachability
steps.body: Make the appliance reachable on the local network. Initial setup and the control center run same-origin on the device.

steps.title: Sign in locally and verify
steps.body: Log in on the appliance, then use the read-only overview with real API data. More modules only when the hardware supports them.

operateJourney.heading: Day-to-day after installation
operateJourney.intro: Storage, services, and status work on one host — documented like server operations, not SaaS ops with a foreign control plane.

steps.title: Structure storage
steps.body: Pools, shares, and persistent state under a clear boundary. Changes apply on the device, not in an external admin service.

steps.title: Run host services
steps.body: Start, stop, and monitor systemd-managed processes in the host context — without multi-tenant abstraction.

steps.title: Read status from the appliance
steps.body: Overview and future modules only with real API data. Public pages do not show live values for that.

steps.title: Plan release changes
steps.body: Version changes through published media, documented steps, and release notes — not hidden auto-updates.

release.label: Release status
release.title: Early-access USB appliance image (build + local staging)
release.body: Vyntrio ships a firmware-bootable raw BIOS disk image built from this repository. There is no production CDN yet — operators build locally, stage under distro/release/staging, and optionally expose /release/* from the API host.

pillars.heading: Storage, services, control
pillars.intro: Three layers on one device: disk, processes, administration — no separate cloud layer.

storage.title: State on disk
storage.body: Read-only block device inventory is available in the signed-in dashboard today. Pools, shares, and storage actions remain planned for later releases.

services.title: Processes on the host
services.body: Systemd-managed services on the hardware itself. No multi-tenant SaaS, no foreign control plane.

control.title: Local control center
control.body: After sign-in you get a read-only overview with real API data. Additional modules appear only when the appliance supports them.

useCases.heading: Built for people who run their own host
useCases.intro: Vyntrio OS targets owners of dedicated hardware who want storage, services, and control on the device. These describe the intended users — not a list of shipped features.

cases.title: One host instead of scattered boxes
cases.body: Consolidate storage and host services on a single dedicated machine you administer locally — the appliance model this project is built around.

cases.title: Files and services on owned hardware
cases.body: Keep working data and internal services on a device under your control, not in a foreign cloud or a multi-tenant admin plane.

cases.title: Your data stays on your device
cases.body: A private-cloud alternative for people who prefer their storage and administration to live on hardware they own.

cases.title: A dedicated appliance base
cases.body: A Linux-based appliance foundation for running your own services on the host, with a local control center after sign-in as modules land.

creatorAudience.heading: Built for people who keep work on their own hardware
creatorAudience.intro: Vyntrio is a local-first appliance for creators, homelab builders, and self-hosters who want libraries, projects, and tools on a machine they control — not in a rented cloud admin plane. This describes who the product speaks to and what the current release supports; it is not a shipped feature checklist.
creatorAudience.ariaLabel: Creator and media positioning for Vyntrio OS

cases.title: Libraries that stay on your device
cases.body: Plan photo, video, and audio libraries on owned storage. Today the signed-in dashboard offers disk inventory plus declared pools and share plans; on-disk formatting and SMB/NFS are not available — there is no bundled streaming app in this release.

cases.title: Exports and working sets close to the host
cases.body: Keep render outputs, RAW folders, and project trees on the appliance instead of shuttling them through third-party sync. Local backup status appears in the overview; automated archive workflows are not shipped yet.

cases.title: A base for software you choose to run
cases.body: Vyntrio is a Linux appliance foundation for running your own services on the host. There is no app marketplace or one-click container catalog yet — administration starts in the local WebGUI after sign-in.

cases.title: One local control center for your rack or desk
cases.body: Consolidate administration for a home lab, small studio, or side-project server. Remote management and licensing layers are intentionally deferred until the local product path is solid.

localToday.heading: What you can do locally right now
localToday.intro: The public site describes direction; the appliance shows only what is implemented. After sign-in on a running Vyntrio host you can use the capabilities below — nothing here requires Remote Connect or a license check.

steps.title: Manage from the local WebGUI
steps.body: Sign in on the device to open the read-only control center with real host and service data from the appliance API.

steps.title: Review storage inventory
steps.body: See classified block devices and eligibility reasons in the dashboard. You can declare pools and prepare dataset/share plans; formatting disks and publishing SMB/NFS shares are not available yet.

steps.title: Reach the dashboard on your LAN
steps.body: When the operator enables HTTPS bind on the appliance, browsers on the local network can open the dashboard — there is no remote cloud relay in this release.

steps.title: Know what is still deferred
steps.body: App containers, VMs, advanced NAS workflows, Remote Connect, and licensing pages are not part of the current product surface. Install media is built locally — see the release area for the BIOS image path.

firstBootSetup.heading: What happens when you start the appliance
firstBootSetup.intro: Vyntrio is a local-first appliance. After the device boots, the dashboard is the primary management surface on the machine itself. No remote account, no cloud login, and no feature gates are needed for the first steps.
firstBootSetup.ariaLabel: First boot local setup steps for Vyntrio OS

steps.title: Boot from verified media
steps.body: Start the target device from the verified install medium. The live runtime boots into RAM and starts the local dashboard service automatically.

steps.title: Open the dashboard on the device
steps.body: The dashboard runs on the appliance itself. Open it in a browser on the same machine, or on the LAN when the operator enables HTTPS bind (VYNTRIO_LAN_BIND_IP).

steps.title: Sign in or create the owner account
steps.body: First boot creates the owner account locally. No external identity provider, no cloud sign-up, and no license check before sign-in.

steps.title: Review the local overview
steps.body: After sign-in you see an overview with real data from the appliance. Storage inventory, declared pools, and share plans are available in the dashboard; on-disk format and protocol services come later.

surfaceShowcase.heading: The admin surface at a glance
surfaceShowcase.intro: This is what the control center looks like — static, no live data, same surface as after sign-in.

surfacePreview.heading: Vyntrio Control Center
surfacePreview.panelNote: Instance name, readiness, and modules appear only after successful sign-in to a real appliance.

productStatus.heading: What works today vs what does not
productStatus.body: This is the availability boundary for Vyntrio OS right now — not a marketing wish list.
points: Available: download metadata, USB appliance image when staged, Media Creator tools, owner sign-in, disk inventory, declared pool/share plans
Runtime-proven on appliance media: kernel → initramfs → squashfs → firstboot → local dashboard/WebUI (QEMU UEFI proof)
Not available: on-disk pool format, SMB/NFS/iSCSI, filesystem ACLs, apps/containers, VMs, remote/cloud control
Public pages never invent download links or live metrics

finalCta.heading: Install media and docs
finalCta.body: Get the current image and media creator from Download. Read Docs for build/stage/boot limits. Sign in only on a running appliance.

hero.title: Getting started with Vyntrio Media Creator
hero.description: Download the native desktop creator for your host OS, then write the dual-mode (BIOS + UEFI) USB appliance image to flash media.

platformHero.title: Download Media Creator for your platform
platformHero.description: Install the native Vyntrio Media Creator, choose the current dual-mode release, select USB storage, and write. UEFI support is required for modern hardware.
platformHero.macosBlockedNote: macOS .app/.dmg not produced on this Linux build host (hdiutil/notarization unavailable). Tauri source lives under desktop/vyntrio-media-creator.
platformHero.cardsIntro: Platform packages below appear only when staged on this host. Loopback browser wizards are not native desktop parity.

creatorPreview.heading: Creator flow
creatorPreview.intro: Welcome → release selection → USB target → write/confirm → progress → completion with boot guidance. Original Vyntrio UI preview (not a third-party screenshot).

installMedia.heading: Install image status
installMedia.intro: Primary artifact: dual-mode GPT hybrid disk image (vyntrio-install-media.img) with BIOS + UEFI. BIOS-only media is incomplete. Status is loaded from GET /api/v1/public/install-media on this host.
installMedia.buildIntro: There is no hidden production download. Build the image in this repository, stage it for HTTP serving, and verify the manifest before booting hardware.

mediaWriter.heading: Native Media Creator packages
mediaWriter.intro: Windows: NSIS installer. Linux: .deb and AppImage. Built with Tauri (native desktop WebView). macOS packaging is blocked without a macOS host. CLI writer aliases remain optional.
mediaWriter.honestNote: Withdrawn as native GUI: Go loopback localhost wizard packages. Windows NSIS is unsigned (SmartScreen may warn). AppImage/.deb are unsigned. Elevation required to write USB. Install image is dual-mode BIOS+UEFI.
flowSteps: Download the native Media Creator for your platform when staged.
Open Media Creator: welcome → select release → select USB → confirm → write.
Eject and boot the USB in UEFI (preferred) or BIOS/legacy mode.
mediaWriter.writeCommand:
vyntrio-write-media write --image vyntrio-install-media.img --device <device-from-list> --dry-run
vyntrio-write-media write --image vyntrio-install-media.img --device <device-from-list> --yes
mediaWriter.downloadsIntro: Links appear only for staged files. Native installer kinds are labeled explicitly.
mediaWriter.downloadUnavailable: Not staged on this host — build from source

panel.heading: Artifact status
panel.intro: All entries are static and will be filled with the first public release. There is currently no file to download.
panel.note: On release, filename, checksum, signature status, and release notes appear here. Until then there is no hidden download link.

readiness.heading: What to clarify before your first install attempt
readiness.intro: These points help you judge whether your setup fits the appliance model — whether or not media is published yet.

items.title: Dedicated host
items.description: Target is a physical server or homelab host — not a container-only or VM-only setup as the long-term solution.

items.title: Legacy BIOS boot
items.description: The current image targets BIOS/legacy firmware (raw MBR disk). UEFI-only systems are not supported in this release.

items.title: Console or out-of-band
items.description: First install needs local access to the device — monitor, KVM, or an equivalent path.

items.title: Local network
items.description: Initial setup and the control center run on the LAN, same-origin on the appliance.

items.title: Data on target disks
items.description: Install can overwrite existing data on the target medium. Clarify your backup plan before the first attempt.

mediaCreator.heading: Create bootable install media
mediaCreator.intro: Step-by-step paths to turn the BIOS raw image into bootable USB media or a VM test disk. There is no in-browser writer — use the helper scripts on a machine you control.
mediaCreator.earlyAccessNote: Early-access engineering media: not a production release channel. USB writes are destructive. This is a USB-boot appliance image (OS on the stick); Secure Boot is unsupported.
warnings: Writing to the wrong block device erases that disk permanently.
Use BIOS/legacy firmware only — UEFI-only hosts cannot boot this image.
Helper scripts run on your computer, not in the browser.
Boot is structurally verified on the build host — confirm on your own hardware or VM.
mediaCreator.usbIntro: For a physical server or PC: download or build the image, verify the checksum, then write the entire raw disk image to a USB stick.

usbSteps.title: Get the image
usbSteps.body: Download from this page when staged, or run make install-media then make release-install-media-stage in the repository.

usbSteps.title: Verify SHA-256
usbSteps.body: Compare the checksum above with sha256sum on the file. Do not write if it mismatches.

usbSteps.title: Choose the USB device
usbSteps.body: Use lsblk or your OS disk tool. Unmount partitions and confirm the whole-disk path — not a partition number.

usbSteps.title: Dry-run, then write
usbSteps.body: Run the helper with --dry-run first, then run with sudo and type the device path to confirm.

usbSteps.title: Boot in BIOS mode
usbSteps.body: Select legacy BIOS boot from the USB stick. Do not use UEFI-only boot settings.

mediaCreator.usbHelperCommand:
# dry-run (validates image + checksum)
./scripts/write-install-media-usb.sh --dry-run

# write (destructive — replace /dev/sdX with your device)
sudo ./scripts/write-install-media-usb.sh --device /dev/sdX
mediaCreator.vmIntro: For evaluation without touching a USB stick: attach the .img file as a virtual disk. The image file is read, not overwritten.

vmSteps.title: Get the image
vmSteps.body: Same artifact as bare metal — staged download or local build output.

vmSteps.title: Set firmware to BIOS
vmSteps.body: In QEMU, VirtualBox, or VMware: choose legacy BIOS, not UEFI.

vmSteps.title: Attach as raw disk
vmSteps.body: Point the VM at vyntrio-install-media.img as an IDE/SATA disk. Use --firmware uefi (OVMF) or --firmware bios.

vmSteps.title: Optional helper
vmSteps.body: From the repo, run the VM helper below if qemu-system-x86_64 is installed.

mediaCreator.vmHelperCommand:
# print command without booting
./scripts/run-install-media-vm.sh --dry-run

# boot when qemu is available
./scripts/run-install-media-vm.sh
mediaCreator.afterBootHeading: After the VM or USB boot
mediaCreator.afterBootBody: GRUB loads the live initramfs. Open the dashboard on the appliance (loopback HTTP by default). There is no target-disk install step yet — see docs for first boot and limitations.
mediaCreator.checksumPending: Available after staging on this host
mediaCreator.downloadPending: Build and stage locally — see status section above

installOutline.heading: Planned install workflow
installOutline.intro: This is the path from media to a reachable appliance once published images exist. Until then it orients you on what to expect.
installOutline.ariaLabel: Planned install workflow for Vyntrio OS

steps.title: Download and verify image
steps.body: Fetch the published install image and verify against the listed checksum — no blind download without verification.

steps.title: Write boot media
steps.body: Write the image to USB or equivalent. Boot the device from that medium, not from the existing disk.

steps.title: Run the installer
steps.body: Set EFI boot order, run the installer, and choose the target disk deliberately. Partitioning follows the release — not invented here.

steps.title: Configure network
steps.body: Make the appliance reachable on the LAN. IP, gateway, and DNS are set on the device — not through a foreign admin cloud.

steps.title: First sign-in
steps.body: Sign in locally on the device and open the control center. Overview then shows real API data for the instance.

steps.title: Follow ops docs
steps.body: Use the docs structure for storage, services, and maintenance. Release changes stay tied to published media.

mediaPrep.heading: Prepare boot media
mediaPrep.intro: Once an install image is published, writing and verifying the medium is the first step — before booting the target device.

items.title: Verify before writing
items.description: Check the image file against SHA-256 from the release area. A mismatch means: do not boot.

items.title: USB or equivalent
items.description: Bootable medium with enough capacity for the image. Exact format follows the publication.

items.title: Test EFI boot
items.description: After writing, boot from the medium once before touching production data on the target host.

requirements.heading: Hardware and environment requirements
requirements.intro: Baseline picture for a target appliance device. Concrete limits (RAM, disk size) ship with the release — no invented numbers here.

items.title: x86_64 with UEFI
items.description: 64-bit host with EFI-capable firmware. Primary target: dedicated server or homelab hardware.

items.title: Memory
items.description: RAM needs depend on storage and service load. Binding minimums appear in the release — not before.

items.title: Target disk
items.description: Persistent storage for system and appliance data. Size and layout are documented with the install image.

items.title: Network connection
items.description: Ethernet on the local network for initial setup and day-to-day use. No internet requirement for basic administration.

items.title: Physical access
items.description: Monitor, keyboard, or KVM for first install and recovery — even when much later work happens over the LAN.

inlineCta.heading: Back to product preview
inlineCta.body: Release details stay on this page. For appliance structure context, start on the preview homepage.

productionInlineCta.heading: Continue on the site
productionInlineCta.body: Release details stay on this page. For appliance structure context, start on the homepage.

hero.title: Documentation for install and operations
hero.description: Topics for getting started, installation, day-to-day use, and recovery — static structure, no live search, no hidden release files.

guide.heading: How this documentation is organized
guide.body: Sections follow the path from first install to running operations. Install media and artifact status stay in the release area — not hidden inside docs.

sections.heading: First steps
sections.intro: What to know before and right after install — without front-running step-by-step detail that ships with real media.

items.title: Read the release area
items.description: Artifact status, checksum, and companion docs start at /download — not in a hidden footer link.

items.title: Install workflow
items.description: Steps from boot medium to a reachable appliance — fully documented once the image is published.

items.title: First sign-in
items.description: Local device login and transition into the control center with real API data for the instance.

items.title: Release notes
items.description: Version-specific changes, breaking changes, and known limits — only after an actual release.

sections.heading: Installation in detail
sections.intro: Reference for media, hardware, and initial configuration — complements the workflow in the release area with operations context.

items.title: Boot media and verification
items.description: Write the image, verify checksum, test EFI boot. Do not boot faulty media on a production host.

items.title: Partitioning and target disk
items.description: Which disks are used for system and data follows the release installer — not this overview.

items.title: Network on first boot
items.description: Set IP, gateway, and DNS on the device. Confirm LAN reachability before further steps.

items.title: Post-install checks
items.description: Overview in the control center: instance name, readiness, and supported modules — only after real sign-in.

sections.heading: Appliance in daily use
sections.intro: Storage, services, and control on the host — documented like a server product, not a SaaS dashboard.

items.title: Storage & shares
items.description: Pools, shares, and persistent state under a clear operations boundary on the device.

items.title: Host services
items.description: Manage systemd-driven processes on the hardware — start, stop, dependencies.

items.title: Control center
items.description: Read-only overview and future modules only when the appliance actually provides them.

items.title: Access and sign-in
items.description: Sessions and admin actions run same-origin on the appliance — no separate cloud login.

sections.heading: Recovery and release changes
sections.intro: What applies to problems, backups, and version changes — without promising unimplemented recovery tools.

items.title: Backup before changes
items.description: Back up configuration and data before changing storage layout or release version.

items.title: Recovery media
items.description: Whether and how recovery images ship with a release is stated in release notes — not before.

items.title: Release upgrade
items.description: Version changes only through published media and documented steps — no silent background update.

items.title: Public docs boundary
items.description: Live status, metrics, and admin actions appear only after sign-in to a real appliance.

inlineCta.heading: Continue to release preview
inlineCta.body: Documentation and install media stay separate. For artifact status, switch to the release area.

productionInlineCta.heading: Continue to release area
productionInlineCta.body: Documentation and install media stay separate. For artifact status, switch to the release area.

footer.tagline: Vyntrio OS — homelab appliance for storage, services, and control.
footer.previewNote: Design preview Block 11R.9 — lazy-loaded, not in the `/` bundle
